Unbelief is a sin that easily troubles even good people when there are conflicts outside and fears within; and it is difficult to overcome them. Lord, increase our faith! We may be ashamed to think that the words of a Philistine would go further than the words of an Israelite, and that the city of Gath would be a place of refuge for a good man when the cities of Israel refuse him a safe home. David gained a comfortable settlement, not only far from Gath but bordering on Israel, where he could keep in touch with his own people.

While David was in the land of the Philistines, he attacked some remnants of the devoted nations. The people whom he cut off were long ago doomed to destruction. It is often wise to avoid public attention, but we must never be idle in any situation. We should always try to do something for God. David hid this expedition from Achish. But a deception that serves the purpose of a lie is as much like it as a hypocrite is to an unholy person; it is only better in appearance, therefore more dangerous. Yet, even though believers often show imperfections, they cannot be persuaded to abandon service to God, or join interests with his enemies, or finally become servants of sin and Satan. But what a series of evils follow from unbelief! When we forget the Lord’s past kindnesses and gracious assurances, we will be overwhelmed by despairing fears and probably be led to adopt some dishonorable way to get rid of our troubles. Nothing can so effectively establish us in holy dispositions and practices and preserve us from difficulties as firm, unwavering dependence on God's promises in Christ Jesus.
